subject: Miranda v. Arizona (1966) and the Miranda warning
central_question: Why do police in the United States have to read you your rights, and what invisible system does that 60-second ritual actually protect?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how one 1966 Supreme Court decision turned a constitutional principle into a scripted police ritual that quietly governs every American arrest.
surprise: The warning is not really about being polite to suspects: it is a structural fix the Court imposed because the interrogation room itself was the problem.
stakes: Whether a confession can be used against you, when police must stop questioning, and how the balance between police power and individual rights is enforced in practice.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in law, history and how systems work
demand_evidence: The phrase 'you have the right to remain silent' has extremely high familiarity and search volume in the US.
Landmark-case explainers sustain durable long-tail interest.
competition: Existing videos mostly re-tell the case facts; few center the 'why this became a system' causal angle.
differentiation: Frames Miranda as a structural remedy (the hidden system), not a courtroom drama, and burns in primary-source citations for major claims.
research_feasibility: High: the opinion is public domain (384 U.S. 436); CourtListener and Oyez provide the full opinion, syllabus and oral-argument records.
visual_feasibility: High: symbolic stills (interrogation room, the warning card), animated diagrams of the Fifth/Sixth Amendment chain, timeline of the four consolidated cases.
risk_notes: Decided landmark case (1966); parties deceased/institutional; opinion is public domain - advertiser-friendly.
Editorial ethics (docs/32): describe Ernesto Miranda as charged/convicted accurately; never state allegations as fact.
Generated visuals are illustration/reconstruction, never presented as authentic historical footage (invariant 11).
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding and key facts to the primary opinion plus one independent reference.
